concepts.title: API Manager: Instances, Autodiscovery & the Gateway

card.what: API Manager governs APIs at runtime: an API instance (created from an Exchange spec or as a basic endpoint) gets an API ID; the Mule app pairs with it via autodiscovery, turning the embedded gateway in the runtime into the policy enforcement point.
card.how: Add API to API Manager → choose managing type (Basic endpoint / Endpoint with proxy / direct app pairing). The app declares api:autodiscovery apiId="${api.id}" flowRef="main-flow"; the runtime authenticates to the platform with environment client_id/client_secret (anypoint.platform.client_id/secret properties) and downloads policies/contracts/SLAs, enforcing them in-process. Alternatively deploy an auto-generated PROXY app between consumers and the implementation.
exam_traps: Autodiscovery pairing needs the ENVIRONMENT client credentials configured on the runtime (anypoint.platform.client_id/secret) — without them the API stays 'unregistered' and policies silently don't apply. Top troubleshooting question.
API status 'Active' in API Manager + green app in Runtime Manager are INDEPENDENT — an app can run fine while tracking/policies are broken.
Proxy vs direct: proxies add a hop but decouple (good for non-Mule backends / strict separation); direct autodiscovery embeds enforcement (no extra hop). Know when each is the 'recommended' answer.
Deleting/recreating an API instance changes the API ID — the app's property must update; stale IDs = no enforcement.
card.hands_on: Publish a spec to Exchange, create an API instance (direct pairing), wire api:autodiscovery with the apiId property, set platform client credentials in the runtime config, deploy, and watch the instance turn green ('Active'). Apply a rate-limit policy and verify 429s. Break the credentials and observe silent non-enforcement — burn that failure mode into memory.
card.deep_dive:
Gateway capability per target: Mule runtime (embedded), Flex Gateway (Envoy-based, for any backend, connected/local modes), Service Mesh (legacy K8s sidecar story). Policy download is periodic — offline runtimes keep last-known policies.

Environment promotion: API instances exist per environment; promote configuration between envs via API Manager promotion or platform APIs/CLI in CI/CD. Labeling instances per env+version and keeping IDs in properties per environment is operational hygiene exams reference.

API Instances expose autodiscovery for Mule, or upstream/downstream config for Flex — consolidated 'API Administration' UX in newer platform versions.

quiz.q: Policies show as applied in API Manager but requests are clearly not being rate-limited. First thing to check?
options: Runtime's anypoint.platform.client_id/secret and the apiId pairing (autodiscovery registration)
quiz.explanation: Unpaired apps enforce nothing — and fail silently. Credentials + apiId are the pairing keys.

quiz.q: You must govern a legacy .NET API with MuleSoft policies without touching its code. Best fit?
options: A Mule proxy app or Flex Gateway in front of it, managed by API Manager
quiz.explanation: Proxy/Flex Gateway are the enforcement points for non-Mule backends.

options: The flow whose HTTP listener serves the API traffic (typically the APIkit main flow)
quiz.explanation: Enforcement wraps the listener of the referenced flow — pointing elsewhere governs nothing.

card.what: Policies are reusable enforcement units injected around API flows: security (OAuth/JWT/Client ID/IP allowlist), quality-of-service (rate limiting, spike control, caching), transformation (header injection/removal), and troubleshooting (message logging). Custom policies extend the catalog.
card.how: Applied per API instance (or automated org/env-wide via Automated Policies). Each policy declares an ORDER — lower executes first inbound; security policies should precede QoS (reject unauthenticated traffic before it consumes rate-limit quota). Rate limiting = fixed windows per identifier (e.g., client_id); Spike Control = smooths bursts with a rolling window + queuing/delay; SLA-based rate limiting ties windows to the consumer's contracted tier.
exam_traps: Rate Limiting vs Spike Control: RL protects per-consumer fairness over windows (429 on exceed, SLA-aware), Spike Control protects the BACKEND from bursts (can delay/queue briefly). Choosing which protects 'my fragile backend' = Spike Control.
Policy ORDER matters: rate-limit before auth = attackers burn your windows; the recommended order places authentication first. Expect an ordering question.
SLA-based policies REQUIRE Client ID enforcement context (the client_id identifies the tier) — SLA rate limiting without client identification cannot work.
Automated policies apply org/business-group/environment-wide and CANNOT be removed per-API by app teams — governance hammer; know the precedence.
card.hands_on: Apply to one API: Client ID enforcement (order 1), SLA-based rate limiting with two tiers (Free 10/min auto-approved, Premium 1000/min manual approval), spike control (e.g., 50 req/s). Register two consumer apps on different tiers and demonstrate differentiated 429 behavior with a load script.
card.deep_dive:
Custom policies: built (HTTP policy project with Mule XML + YAML definition) and published to Exchange, then applicable like any policy — for org-specific concerns (custom auth headers, canonical error bodies, legacy signature schemes). Flex Gateway policies can also be custom (Rust/WASM or PDK).

Resource-level policies: apply to specific methods/paths via patterns — e.g., POST-only throttling.

Caching policy (HTTP caching directives) vs Cache scope in flows (any payload, OS-backed) — different layers. Message logging policy for payload sampling at the edge with obfuscation. Policy + contracts data feeds API Analytics: per-client usage, policy violations — the analytics-driven SLA management loop is a favored scenario.

quiz.q: The backend database collapses under sudden traffic bursts even though consumers stay within monthly quotas. Which policy targets THIS problem?
quiz.explanation: Spike Control smooths instantaneous bursts protecting the backend; rate limiting handles per-consumer fairness over windows.

quiz.q: Correct inbound policy ordering for: JWT validation, rate limiting?
options: JWT validation first, so unauthenticated requests never consume quota
quiz.explanation: Authenticate, then meter. The reverse lets anonymous floods exhaust legitimate consumers' windows.

quiz.q: SLA-based rate limiting requires which companion mechanism?
options: Client ID identification (enforcement) so the consumer's tier can be resolved
quiz.explanation: Tiers attach to client applications via contracts; without client identity there is no tier to apply.

concepts.title: SLA Tiers, Contracts & the Consumer Lifecycle

card.what: SLA tiers define consumption levels (requests/window, approval mode). Consumers discover the API in Exchange, register an application, request access on a tier → a CONTRACT binds app+API+tier. Policies enforce; analytics attribute usage per contract.
card.how: Define tiers on the API instance (name, limit(s), auto/manual approval). Consumer flow: Exchange portal → Request Access → select/create client app → tier → (approval) → receives client_id/secret. The SLA rate-limit policy reads the contract at enforcement. Revoking a contract or rotating credentials cuts that consumer alone. Tier changes/approvals are auditable governance events.
exam_traps: Contracts bind a CLIENT APP to an API INSTANCE in an environment — the same app can hold contracts to many APIs; deleting the instance orphans contracts (re-request needed).
Manual-approval tiers gate production consumers (B2B), auto-approval suits internal/sandbox — picking approval mode per audience is a design question.
Credentials identify the APP; secrets rotation policies and not embedding client_secret in browsers (use OAuth instead for SPAs) are evaluation points.
429 behavior with multiple limits per tier: ANY exceeded window rejects.
card.hands_on: Create Free (auto, 10/min) and Partner (manual, 5000/min) tiers. From a second (consumer) account flow: discover in Exchange, request Partner access, approve from the provider side, call with the issued credentials, exceed the limit and capture the 429 + headers (X-RateLimit-*).
card.deep_dive:
Multi-window tiers (e.g., 100/sec AND 100k/day) compose burst + volume control. Tier migration: upgrades/downgrades take effect on contract update — communicate via the portal.

B2B onboarding playbook: public portal page → self-service request → manual approval workflow → credentials issued → usage visible in analytics → renewal/rotation policy. This lifecycle (and its automation via Platform APIs) is the 'API as a product' operating model the governance questions circle around.

Monetization-ready data: per-contract analytics (requests, violations) export to billing/reporting via Anypoint Analytics APIs.

quiz.q: A partner's integration is compromised. Fastest contained remediation?
options: Revoke that partner app's contract / rotate its credentials — other consumers unaffected
Add an IP blocklist for everyone
quiz.explanation: Per-contract blast radius is the point of consumer-level credentials.

quiz.q: Tier with limits 100/sec AND 50,000/day. A client at 20/sec hits 50,001 requests at 6pm. Result?
quiz.explanation: Multiple windows are AND-ed for admission.

quiz.q: Which approval mode fits a public developer program's sandbox tier vs its production tier?
options: Auto for sandbox; manual for production
Manual for sandbox; auto for production
quiz.explanation: Frictionless trial, gated production — the standard product posture.

card.what: Three governance feedback loops: API Analytics (traffic/performance/policy-violation metrics per API and consumer), API Manager + Runtime Manager alerts (threshold-driven notifications), and Anypoint API Governance (design-time conformance rulesets validating Exchange specs continuously).
card.how: Analytics dashboards slice requests by API, client, policy violations, response codes/times; custom reports export via Analytics APIs. API alerts: e.g., policy-violation count, request count, response time thresholds → email/notification channels. Governance: apply rulesets (OAS best practices, naming, security requirements — e.g., 'all APIs must define 401/403 responses') to Exchange asset groups; conformance reports + notifications drive spec quality without manual review.
exam_traps: API Analytics measures GATEWAY-level traffic (managed APIs) — apps without API Manager pairing produce no API analytics (Monitoring still sees app metrics). The 'why is analytics empty' answer is usually missing autodiscovery.
Governance rulesets validate SPECS at design time, not runtime traffic — confusing them with runtime policies is the planted error.

Alerts exist in BOTH API Manager (API-centric: violations, response time per API instance) and Runtime Manager (app-centric: CPU, memory, deployment events) — questions ask which side owns which alert.
Conformance ≠ enforcement: a non-conformant spec still deploys unless your CI gates on the governance API.
card.hands_on: Generate traffic (mixed valid/invalid credentials) against your governed API. In Analytics: chart requests by client app and policy violations. Create an alert: >10 policy violations in 5 minutes → email. In API Governance, apply the 'OpenAPI Best Practices' ruleset to your spec group and fix one real finding it reports.
card.deep_dive:
Metrics taxonomy: API Analytics (request counts, latency percentiles per API/client/policy) vs Anypoint Monitoring (app/infra: CPU, memory, custom dashboards, log search) vs Visualizer (dependency graph from actual traffic). MCIA expects you to name the right tool per question.

Governance-as-code: rulesets are Exchange assets (Spectral-based); custom rules encode org standards (auth mandatory, kebab-case paths, info.contact present). CI integration via governance API/CLI gates PRs on conformance — 'shift-left' governance.

Operational KPI design: per-API SLOs (p95 latency, error rate) with alert thresholds wired to on-call — the maturity story examiners want articulated.

quiz.q: API Analytics shows zero data for a high-traffic Mule app. Most likely cause?
options: The app isn't paired to an API instance (no autodiscovery) — gateway metrics never emit
quiz.explanation: No API instance pairing → no API-level analytics, regardless of traffic volume.

quiz.q: Mandate: every API spec in the org must declare OAuth security and standard error responses, checked automatically on every publish. Tool?
options: API Governance rulesets applied to Exchange asset groups
quiz.explanation: Design-time conformance = Governance rulesets. Automated policies are runtime enforcement.

quiz.q: Which alert belongs to Runtime Manager rather than API Manager?
quiz.explanation: Infrastructure/app health = Runtime Manager; API traffic/policy metrics = API Manager.

side_quests.title: Govern the Ungovernable
side_quests.description: Stand up a Flex Gateway (local mode, Docker) in front of any non-Mule API (a tiny Express/json-server app). Register it, apply rate limiting + an auth policy from API Manager, and prove enforcement with curl. You now govern a non-Mule API with the same control plane — write 5 lines on when you'd choose this over a Mule proxy.

side_quests.title: The Conformance Crusade
side_quests.description: Author a custom governance ruleset with 3 org rules (security scheme mandatory, version in path, contact info present), apply it to your Exchange specs, and produce a conformance report. Fix violations until 100%.

boss.title: The Governance Lord
boss.intro: The Governance Lord rules a federation of a thousand APIs. Unpaired apps, unordered policies, and tierless consumers are exiled from his realm. Show him an API operated as a product.

mcq.q: A new API must enforce: JWT auth, 100 req/min per consumer by tier, burst protection for the backend. Correct policy set + order?
options: Spike control → SLA rate limit → JWT
JWT validation → SLA-based rate limiting (with Client ID context) → Spike Control
mcq.explanation: Authenticate first, meter per consumer second, smooth aggregate bursts last before the backend.

mcq.q: Ops reports the API 'Active' but a JUST-APPLIED policy isn't enforcing on one of three on-prem runtimes (its network to the control plane is flaky). Explanation?
options: That runtime hasn't downloaded the latest policy set; it enforces last-known policies until connectivity restores
mcq.explanation: Policy distribution is pull-based; disconnected gateways keep prior state — eventual consistency of governance.

mcq.q: Security demands EVERY API in the production environment logs requests and enforces TLS-version minimums — including future APIs, no exceptions, no per-team opt-out. Mechanism?
options: Automated Policies at the environment level
mcq.explanation: Automated policies apply to all (current and future) APIs in scope and can't be removed by API owners.

mcq.q: An internal team bypasses the gateway by calling the implementation's CloudHub URL directly. Architectural remediations? (best answer)
options: Lock the implementation to accept only gateway-originated traffic (VPC/private space + LB rules, or mTLS between gateway and impl), making the managed endpoint the only path
mcq.explanation: Governance is only real if the gateway is unavoidable — network/identity controls close the side door.

mcq.q: Which data source answers 'which consumer caused yesterday's 429 storm on orders-api v2'?
options: API Analytics filtered by API instance, client application, and policy-violation outcome
mcq.explanation: Per-contract attribution lives in API Analytics — the operational payoff of client ID enforcement.

scenario.prompt:
BUILD TASK — Rule the Realm:

Productize one API end-to-end:
1. Spec in Exchange with governance ruleset conformance at 100%.
2. API instance with autodiscovery pairing (prove Active status + enforcement).
3. Policies in correct order: JWT (or Client ID) → SLA rate limiting (2 tiers) → Spike Control; document the order rationale in the README.
4. Two consumer apps on different tiers with contracts; load-test showing differentiated 429s + X-RateLimit headers.
5. One API Manager alert (policy violations) + one Runtime Manager alert (CPU) firing into your inbox.
6. Analytics screenshot-equivalent: requests by client app for the test window.
checklist: Conformance report clean; spec is the single source of truth
Silent-non-enforcement failure mode tested (break pairing creds, observe, restore)
Policy ordering correct and justified in writing
Tier differentiation proven under load with captured 429 evidence
Both alert types received; analytics attribute traffic per consumer correctly
